sections.heading: Why Standard Litter Boxes Fail Big Cats
sections.content:
Standard litter boxes measure around 18" x 14" — designed for an average 10-pound cat. But Maine Coons weigh 15-25 pounds and measure 30-40 inches from nose to tail. Ragdolls, Norwegian Forest Cats, and British Shorthairs face similar sizing problems.

A too-small box creates three issues:

**Overflow and mess.** Large cats can't dig and cover properly in a tight box. Litter gets kicked over the sides, urine hits the rim instead of the litter, and waste lands in corners the cat can't reach.

**Discomfort and avoidance.** Cats who can't turn around comfortably associate the box with stress. Over time, they seek alternatives — your carpet, bathtub, or laundry basket.

**Joint strain.** Squeezing into a small box stresses joints, particularly problematic for large breeds already prone to hip dysplasia.

The fix is simple: **size up.** Your cat's litter box interior should be at least 1.5x their body length. For a 25-pound Maine Coon measuring 36 inches, that's a 24"+ interior minimum.

sections.heading: Size Guide: What Dimensions Your Big Cat Actually Needs
sections.content:
Based on veterinary recommendations and real-world testing:

**Cats 12-16 lbs (large domestic breeds):**
- Minimum interior: 20" x 16"
- Recommended: 22" x 18"
- Most standard enclosures work at this size

**Cats 16-22 lbs (Maine Coon, Ragdoll):**
- Minimum interior: 24" x 18"
- Recommended: 26" x 20"
- Need oversized enclosures or open jumbo pans

**Cats 22+ lbs (large male Maine Coons, multi-breed large cats):**
- Minimum interior: 26" x 20"
- Recommended: 28"+ x 20"+
- Only the widest enclosures (39"+) or jumbo open pans work

The **depth matters too.** Large cats kick more litter, so you need at least 6 inches of litter depth — which means high-sided pans or enclosed boxes that contain the spray.

For breed-specific recommendations, see our [Maine Coon litter box guide](/guides/best-litter-box-maine-coons/).

sections.heading: Best Overall: Enclosure with Drawers & Storage (39")
sections.content:
**Price: $350.99** | Interior width: 39"

This is the widest furniture enclosure in our lineup, and it's the only one we recommend without reservation for cats over 20 pounds. The 39-inch interior accommodates jumbo litter pans that most enclosures simply can't fit.

The **anti-tip kit** is especially important here — a 25-pound cat jumping off the top of the enclosure generates significant force. The wall anchor prevents toppling.

The built-in drawers are a practical bonus: jumbo litter pans consume more litter, so having bags stored right there saves trips to the closet.

**Key specs for large cats:**
- Interior fits pans up to 28" x 20"
- Anti-tip wall anchor included
- Moisture-resistant sealed interior
- Sturdy engineered wood handles heavy cats

See this product in our full [best cat litter box roundup](/guides/best-cat-litter-box-2026/).

sections.heading: Best Automatic: 60L Smart Self-Cleaning Box
sections.content:
**Price: $268.99** | Capacity: 60L | Max cat weight: 18 lbs

The 60-liter capacity handles cats up to 18 pounds, which covers most large domestic cats and smaller Maine Coons. The self-cleaning mechanism is a genuine relief for large-cat owners — oversized waste is harder to scoop and produces more odor.

The **infrared sensor** pauses cleaning if a cat enters during the cycle, and the **app tracks usage patterns** — useful for monitoring whether your large cat is visiting regularly or avoiding the box.

**Limitation:** The 18-lb weight limit means this won't work for very large Maine Coons (20+ lbs). For those cats, the 39" furniture enclosure with a manual jumbo pan is the better choice.

For more automatic options, see our [self-cleaning litter box guide](/guides/best-self-cleaning-litter-boxes/).

sections.heading: High-Sided vs Enclosed: What Works Better for Big Cats
sections.content:
Large cats kick more litter and produce higher-volume waste. You need containment — but the type matters.

**High-sided open pans** (8"+ sides) prevent most litter scatter while keeping the top open for easy access and airflow. They're the simplest solution and easiest to clean. But they don't hide the box or control odor.

**Enclosed furniture** hides everything, reduces tracking, and contains odor — but the interior needs to be large enough for your cat to turn around. That's where most enclosures fail big cats: the interior is designed for 10-pound cats.

Our recommendation: **Use an enclosed furniture enclosure (39"+) as the primary box, and a high-sided open jumbo pan as the secondary box.** This gives your cat options and ensures one box is always easy to access.

If you're deciding between covered and uncovered styles more broadly, our [hooded vs open litter box guide](/guides/hooded-vs-open-litter-box/) covers the research on cat preferences.

sections.heading: Litter Depth and Type for Large Cats
sections.content:
Large cats need more litter — both depth and volume:

**Depth:** 4-6 inches minimum. Large cats dig more aggressively and need enough depth to bury waste completely. Too shallow, and clumps hit the bottom of the pan, making them harder to scoop.

**Volume:** A jumbo pan with 6 inches of litter uses roughly 30-40 pounds of litter per month per box. Budget accordingly — multi-cat homes with large breeds can use 80+ pounds monthly.

**Best litter types for large cats:**
- **Clumping clay (unscented):** Best overall performance, forms tight clumps that don't break apart during heavy digging
- **Walnut shell litter:** Lighter weight, good clumping, less dust (good for large cats in enclosed furniture)
- **Avoid lightweight/pellet litter:** Large cats scatter it more easily, and it doesn't clump as tightly

Regardless of litter type, scoop at least once daily — twice daily if you have [multiple cats sharing boxes](/guides/best-litter-boxes-multi-cat/).

sections.heading: Common Issues with Large Cats and Litter Boxes
sections.content:
**Problem: Cat hangs over the edge.** The box is too small. Size up immediately — your cat should be able to squat fully inside the box without any body part extending over the rim.

**Problem: Litter everywhere despite enclosed box.** Check the entry point. If the entry is at floor level, litter falls out as the cat exits. Enclosures with raised entries or walk-through rooms (like the 2-room wooden enclosure) force the cat to step up, shaking loose litter off their paws.

**Problem: Cat avoids the box entirely.** Rule out medical issues first (UTI, crystals), then assess box size. A cat who suddenly stops using a properly sized box may have outgrown it during a growth spurt — weigh and measure your cat.

**Problem: Enclosure tips when cat jumps on/off.** Use the anti-tip kit, or anchor the enclosure to a wall. Large cats generate enough force to topple unsecured furniture, especially when leaping from height.

buyingCriteria.title: Extra Large Litter Box Buying Criteria

criteria.name: Interior Size
criteria.description: Minimum 1.5x your cat's body length. For 20+ lb cats, look for 24" x 18" or larger interior dimensions.

criteria.name: Side Height
criteria.description: 8"+ sides for open pans to prevent spray and scatter. Enclosed furniture should contain all splash.

criteria.name: Weight Capacity
criteria.description: Both the box and any furniture enclosure should handle your cat's weight plus 50% (for jumping on/off). Anti-tip kits are essential.

criteria.name: Entry Size
criteria.description: Large cats need wider entry holes (8"+ diameter or width). Check that your cat can enter without squeezing.

commonMistakes.mistake: Assuming your cat will adapt to a standard-sized box
commonMistakes.whyItMatters: Large cats don't adapt — they compensate. They'll hang over the edge, skip covering waste, or find a larger alternative (your bathtub). Size the box to the cat, not the other way around.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using lightweight or pellet litter in a jumbo box
commonMistakes.whyItMatters: Large cats dig more forcefully, scattering lightweight litter everywhere. Clumping clay or walnut shell litters stay in place better and form tighter clumps in deep-fill boxes.

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting to anchor large litter box furniture
commonMistakes.whyItMatters: A 20-pound cat launching off a furniture enclosure generates enough force to tip it. Anti-tip kits or wall anchors are essential safety measures, not optional extras.

faq.question: What size litter box does a 20-pound cat need?
faq.answer: A 20-pound cat (typically 28-34 inches long) needs a litter box with interior dimensions of at least 24" x 18". For optimal comfort, 26" x 20" or larger is recommended. Our 39-inch wide enclosure fits the largest jumbo pans available.

faq.question: Can Maine Coons use regular litter boxes?
faq.answer: Most regular litter boxes are too small for Maine Coons. Male Maine Coons average 18-25 pounds and 30-40 inches long — they need jumbo-sized boxes with at least 24" x 18" interiors. Standard boxes lead to overflow, discomfort, and box avoidance.

faq.question: What's the biggest litter box enclosure available?
faq.answer: The largest furniture-style enclosure we've tested is the Drawers & Storage model at 39" x 18" x 31.5". This accommodates jumbo pans up to 28" x 20" — large enough for even the biggest domestic cats.

faq.question: How much litter do I need for an extra large box?
faq.answer: Fill to 4-6 inches of depth. A jumbo pan at this depth uses approximately 25-35 pounds of litter. Plan for one full bag (40 lbs) to fill the pan initially, then top off weekly. Monthly litter usage for one jumbo box runs 30-40 pounds.

faq.question: Are high-sided litter boxes better for big cats?
faq.answer: Yes, for open pans. High sides (8"+) prevent spray and litter scatter without restricting overhead space. For enclosed furniture enclosures, the walls handle containment naturally — but ensure the entry is low enough for easy access, especially for senior large cats.

faq.question: Do extra large litter boxes cost more to maintain?
faq.answer: Yes — larger boxes use more litter (30-40 lbs/month vs 15-20 lbs for standard), and replacement pans (if plastic) are more expensive. But the cost of treating stress-related UTIs or replacing urine-damaged flooring far exceeds the extra litter expense.
